concepts.title: Local Maxima and Minima
concepts.conceptBody:
Local Maxima & Minima 

There may be points in the domain of the function where function does not attain the greatest (or the least) value but its value it greater than or less than the values of function at neighboring points.

Such points are known as local maxima or local minima or combined known as extreme points. The values at these points are called local maximum value, local minimum value and extreme values respectively.

Let us take the following diagram to understand the concept.

Points P, R T are points of local maxima

Points Q, S are points of local minima.

Points to notice: 

  1. All the points of local maxima / minima are critical points i.e. tangent to the curve at these points is parallel to axis or $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ at these points.
  2. They are local maxima / minima, but remember they are not the maximum & minimum values of the functions. Local minimum might not be the least value of function.
  3. A value of local minimum can be greater than value of local maximum. For example in our case value at $\text{S}$ (local minimum value) is greater than value at $\text{P}$ (local maximum value).

concepts.title: Using the First Order derivative test to find the existence of local maxima and minima
concepts.conceptBody:
Steps to find local maxima & local minima 

Method 1 : First derivative test 

Step 1: Find the critical points of the function by putting ${f}'\left( x \right)=0$

Step 2: For each of the critical points obtained in step 1 do the following:

for a critical point $\text{x}=\text{a}$

Case $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ : $x=a$ is local maxima

 

If ${f}'\left( x \right)$ changes from + to - as $x$ passes through a i.e. at every point to the left of a ${f}'\left( x \right)>0~\And $ at every point to the right ${f}'\left( x \right)<0$

Case$2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x=a$ is local minima

if the sign of ${f}'\left( x \right)$ changes from - to + as $x$ passes through a i.e. at every point to the left of $\text{a},\text{f'}\left( \text{x} \right)<\And $ at every point to the right of a, $f'\left( x \right)>0$ 
 

Case 3 : There is no sign change across a this means that $x=a$ is neither $a$ point of maxima or minima.

concepts.title: Using the Higher Order derivative tests to locate the points of local maxima,local minima and inflection at crictical points
concepts.conceptBody:
Higher Order Test 

Let $\text{f}$ be a differentiable function on interval I & let $\text{c}$ be any point in the domain of $\text{f}$ such that

(1) $\text{f'}\left( \text{c} \right)=\text{{f}''}\left( \text{c} \right)=\text{{f}'''}\left( \text{c} \right)\ldots .{{\text{f}}^{\text{n}-1}}\left( \text{c} \right)=0$ and

(2) ${{\text{f}}^{~\text{n}}}\left( \text{c} \right)\ne 0$ and exists.

Then

if $n$ is even $\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{f}}^{\text{n}}}\left( \text{c} \right)<0\Rightarrow \text{x}=\text{c } \text{ is } \text{ a } \text{ local } \text{ maxima }  \\ {{\text{f}}^{\text{n}}}\left( \text{c} \right)>0\Rightarrow \text{x}=\text{c } \text{ is } \text{ a } \text{ local } \text{ minima }  \\ \end{array} \right\}$

$\And$ if $\text{n}$ is odd $\Rightarrow \text{x}=\text{c}$ is a point of inflection.

for example, to understand it better

if $x={{c}_{1}}$ is the root of $f'\left( x \right)$

if $\text{ f''}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)<0$ then $\text{x}={{\text{c}}_{1}}$ is local maxima

$>0$ then $x={{c}_{1}}$ is local minima

$=0$, then we will find $\text{{f}'''}\left( \text{x} \right)$

If $\text{f'''}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)\ne 0$ then $\text{x}={{\text{c}}_{1}}$ is a point of inflection

$\And  \text{ f'''}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)=0$, then we will find ${{\text{f}}^{~\text{IV}}}$.

if ${{\text{f}}^{\text{IV}}}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)<0$, then $\text{x}={{\text{c}}_{1}}$ is local maxima.... & this cycle goes on.

Remember we talk about maxima & minima for even derivatives of $f$ whereas for point of inflection we look for odd derivatives of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$.

concepts.title: Global Maxima and Minima
concepts.conceptBody:
Global Maxima & Minima 

We earlier discussed local maxima & minima which were restricted to their neighborhood. Now we need to find the (greatest / least) of the function over its whole domain. That value is known as global maxima / absolute maxima or global minima / absolute minima.

concepts.title: Steps followed to find the Global Maxima/Minima
concepts.conceptBody:
Steps to find Global Maxima / Minima 

Step 1: Find all critical points. Let say ${{c}_{1}}{{c}_{2}}\ldots {{c}_{n}}$ are $n$ critical points.

Step $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ : Find the value of the function at all these critical points.

Step 3: For maximum $=\text{max}\left\{ f\left( a \right),f\left( {{c}_{1}} \right),f\left( {{c}_{2}} \right)\ldots f\left( {{c}_{n}} \right),f\left( b \right) \right\}$

For minimum $=\text{min}\left\{ \text{f}\left( \text{a} \right),\text{f}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right),\text{f}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)\ldots \text{f}\left( {{\text{c}}_{\text{n}}} \right),\text{f}\left( \text{b} \right) \right\}$

Note: The introduction of end points of domain. As told to you earlier also, the function attains extreme values at critical points or at end points.

concepts.title: Determining the number and nature of roots in a Cubic or Higher Order Polynomial
concepts.conceptBody:
Application of Derivative in Determining the Nature of Roots of a Cubic Polynomial 

Let $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{x}}^{3}}+a{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{bx}+\text{c}$ be the given cubic polynomial, and $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ be the corresponding cubic equation, where $a,b,c\in R$

Now, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{f}'\left( x \right)=3{{x}^{2}}+2ax+b$

Let $D=4{{a}^{2}}-12b=4\left( {{a}^{2}}-3b \right)$, be the discriminate of the equation ${f}'\left( x \right)=0$.

 

Then the following cases arises,

Case I: If D <$0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0 \forall \mathrm{x} \mathbf{R}$.

That means $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ would be an increasing function of $\text{x}$.

Also $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \infty }}f\left( -x \right)=-\infty $ and $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \infty }}f\left( x \right)=\infty ,f$

It is clear that graph of $y=f\left( x \right)$ would cut the $x$-axis only once.

That means we would have just one real root, ( say ${{\text{x}}_{0}}$ ).

Case II: Clearly ${{x}_{0}}>0$ if $c<0$ and ${{x}_{0}}<0$ if $c>0$

If $\mathbf{D}>0,\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ would have two real roots $\left( {} \right.$ say ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{x}}_{2}}$, let ${{\text{x}}_{1}}<{{\text{x}}_{2}}$ )

 

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=3\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)$

Clearly, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)<0,\text{x}\in \left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)$

${f}'\left( x \right)>0,x\left( -\infty ,{{x}_{1}} \right)\cup \left( {{x}_{2}},\infty \right)$

That means $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ would increase in $\left( -\infty ,{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},\infty \right)$ and would decrease in $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)$. Hence, $\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{1}}$ would be a point of local maxima and and $\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{2}}$ would be a point of local minima.

Thus the graph of $y=f\left( x \right)$ could have these five possibilities 

Here $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ with three $f\left( x \right)=0$ with one real root

 

Distinct real roots $\text{x}=\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma $ $\text{x}=\alpha $ and two imaginary root (iii) (iv) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ with one real root $f\left( x \right)=0$ with three roots

$\text{x}=\alpha $ and two imaginary roots $\text{x}=\alpha ,{{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{x}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right.$ being repeated root)

(v)

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ with three real roots

$\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{1}},\alpha \left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right.$ being repeated root)

Thus following results are obtained from above graphs,

(a) $f\left( {{x}_{1}} \right)f\left( {{x}_{2}} \right)>0,f\left( x \right)=0$ would have just one real root.

(b) $f\left( {{x}_{1}} \right)f\left( {{x}_{2}} \right)<0$, $f\left( x \right)=0$ would have three real and distinct roots.

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)=0,\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ would have three real roots but one of the root would be repeated.

Case III : If $\mathbf{D}=0,\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=3{{\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}$ where ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ is root of $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)}^{3}}+\text{k}$

Then $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ has three real roots, if $\text{k}=0$

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ have one real root, if $\text{k}\ne 0$

concepts.title: Curve Tracing
concepts.conceptBody:
Curve Sketching

There are 5 steps to draw a curve, $y=f\left( x \right)$

Step 1 : 

Check for Symmetry

  1. Check whether function is even. 
    (a) Replace $\text{x}$ by $-\text{x}$ 
    (b) If no change in $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$, then function is even 
    (c) Graph is symmetrical about y-axis.
  2. Check if function is odd 
    (a) Replace $\text{x}$ by $-\text{x}$ 
    (b) If $f\left( -x \right)=-f\left( x \right)$, then function is odd 
    (c) Graph is symmetric about origin
  3. Replace $y$ by $-\text{y}$, if no change, then the graph is symmetrical about $\text{x}$-axis.
  4. If function is periodic, draw graph only in the period, as the remaining curve is a repetition of this one.

Step 2: 

Points of Intersection 

(a) Find points of intersection with $\text{x}$-axis & y-axis.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{ for } \text{ x } \text{ axis, } \text{ put } \text{ y}=0 \\ {} & \text{y } \text{ axis, } \text{ put } \text{ x}=0 \\ \end{array}$

(b) Special care of double & triple roots

$\text{ for } \text{ example, } \text{ if } \text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{2}}\text{ g}\left( \text{x} \right)$

here $x=a$, is a double root of $f\left( x \right)$ or you can also say repeated root of $f\left( x \right)=0$ note in this case $\text{f}\left( \text{a} \right)=0,\text{{f}'}\left( \text{a} \right)=0$ but $\text{{f}''}\left( \text{a} \right)\ne 0$ i.e. can be point of local maxima / local minima

 

Now take $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{x}=\text{a}$ is a triple root of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$

here, note

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & f\left( a \right)=0 \\ {} & {f}'\left( a \right)=0 \\ {} & {f}''\left( a \right)=0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ but }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & {f}'''\left( a \right)\ne 0 \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a}$ is the point of inflection (as shown in diagram) i.e. the point where shape of the curve changes from convex to concave & vice versa.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ TIP : if $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{\text{n}}}\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$\And $ nth derivative is the first non-zero derivative then

if $\text{n}$ is even $\to \text{x}=\text{a}$ is a point of local maxima / minima

$\text{n}$ is odd $\to \text{x}=\text{a}$ is a point of inflection.

let us take another example

$\text{y}=\text{x}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}$

we can see that $x=1$ is a double root of $f\left( x \right)$

Step 1 : no symmetry

Step 2: has double root at $\text{x}=1$

Step $3:\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ put $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)>0$

 

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ for $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( 0,\infty \right)\text{y}>0$

$\text{x}\in \left( -\infty 0 \right)~\text{y}<0$

so approximate graph would be

from this we can also deduce that $\text{x}=1$ will be point of local minima & not local maxima. If it was local maxima then the curve will come in the negative half.

Step 3 : 

Sign of Graph 

(a) Put $y>0$ & see where the graph is above x-axis & where it is below x-axis.

 

For example if $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{x}\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}-2 \right)$ put $\text{x}\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}-2 \right)>0$

(using wavy curve)

So corresponding to it the graph will be above $\text{x}$-axis where region is +ve & below $\text{x}$-axis where region is represented by - ve sign.

 

Further $\text{x}\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}-2 \right)$ is a polynomial function, so it will be continuous & hence the following will be the curve.

Check the domain: Find the domain (especially in cases where denominator can become zero). Draw the curve only in its domain.

Step 4: 

Asymptote

Examples of asymptote

 
 

(a) Horizontal asymptote 

  • If $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \infty }}f\left( x \right)={{c}_{1}}$, where ${{c}_{1}}$ is finite then $y={{c}_{1}}$ is horizontal asymptote in right half.
  • if $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to -\infty }}f\left( x \right)={{c}_{2}}$, where ${{c}_{2}}$ is finite then $y={{c}_{2}}$ is horizontal asymptote in left half.

for example

 

$\text{y}=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

here $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\pi /2$ is a horizontal asymptote in right half.

$\text{y}=-\pi /2$ is a horizontal asymptote in left half.

(b) Vertical Asymptote 

  • If $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{y}\to \infty }}\text{g}\left( \text{y} \right)={{\text{c}}_{1}}$, where ${{\text{c}}_{1}}$ is finite, then $\text{x}={{\text{c}}_{1}}$ is a vertical asymptote.

OR

in normal words, if for a finite value of $\text{x}$, $\text{y}$ approaches $(\infty $ or $-\infty )$ then $\text{x}=\text{c}$ is a vertical asymptote.

 

e.g.

$\text{y}=\text{logx}$

$\text{x}=\pi /2$, a vertical asymptote $\text{x}=0$, a vertical asymptote

Take special care in case of

(a) when denominator can be zero. Put denominator $=0$ to get the value of $\text{x}$ for which vertical asymptote will be formed.

(b) Logarithmic functions

for $\text{log}\left( f\left( x \right) \right)=0$, find $f\left( x \right)=1\And $ for those values of $x$ it will be a vertical asymptote.

Step 5: 

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Find the points of maxima, minima.
  •  
  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ find the shape of curve, i.e. either concave or convex.

A twice differentiable function $\text{f}$ defined on interval $\text{I}$ is

(a) concave if and only if ${f}''\left( x \right)\le 0$ for $x\in I$

(b) convex if and only if $\text{{f}''}\left( \text{x} \right)\ge 0$ for $\text{x}\in \text{I}$

inflection is the point where the shape of the curve changes from convex to concave or vice-versa.

concepts.title: Some Solved Examples based on Curve Tracing
concepts.conceptBody:
Trace the following curves 

(a) $y=-3{{x}^{4}}+4{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}-4x$ 

(b) $y=\frac{2x-3}{{{x}^{2}}-3x+2}$ 

Solution: 

(a) The function given is a polynomial which we know are continuous for all $\text{x}$. So there is no point of finding the asymptotes as the function is completely continuous.

We will go step by step for a better understanding

Step 1 : Symmetry: No

There is no symmetry as the function is neither odd nor even.

Step 2: Points of Intersection:

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{y }\!\!~\!\!\text{ intercept }\!\!~\!\!\text{ : }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Put }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=0 \\ {} & \Rightarrow \text{y}=0 \\ \end{array}$

$\text{x}$ intercept: $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Put $\text{y}=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-3{{x}^{4}}+4{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}-4x=0 \\ {} & -{{x}^{3}}\left( 3x-4 \right)+\left( 3x-4 \right)x=0 \\ {} & \left( x-{{x}^{3}} \right)\left( 3x-4 \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & x\left( {{x}^{2}}-1 \right)\left( 3x-4 \right)=0 \\ {} & {} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ xint}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=0,\pm 1,\frac{4}{3} \\ \end{array}$

Step 3 : Sign of curve

Method 1 

now since we have found the intersection points the function can be written as

 

$f\left( x \right)=x\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+1 \right)\left( x-\frac{4}{3} \right)$

by wavy curve

So the curve will lie in

the shaded region

Step 4: Asymptote : Since a continuous function so no asymptotes.

Now nothing is required as we already know everything

The curve will be similar to this shape.

For the maxima & minima points we can find them by differentiating the function.

(b) $y=\left( \frac{2x-3}{{{x}^{2}}-3x+2} \right)$

Now here there is a term in denominator and so we can talk of asymptotes in this question

Step 1 : Symmetry: No

Step 2 : Intersection points & domain

${{\text{y}}_{\text{int }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ put }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=0$

$\begin{matrix} \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{y}_{\text{int }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=-\frac{3}{2} \\ {{x}_{\text{int }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\Rightarrow \left( \frac{2x-3}{{{x}^{2}}-3x+2} \right)=0 \\ \end{matrix}$

Since denominator cannot be zero for a valid fraction

$2\text{x}-3=0$$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{2}$

Step 3 : Sign of function

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & f\left( x \right)=\frac{2x-3}{\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)}=\frac{2x-3}{\left( x-2 \right)\left( x-1 \right)} \\ \end{array}$

The curve will be in these regions

Part-2 

Increasing or decreasing?

Finding $\frac{dy}{dx}$ for it.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \frac{dy}{dx} & ~=\frac{2\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)-\left( 2x-3 \right)\left( 2x-3 \right)}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{\left( 2{{x}^{2}}-6x+4 \right)-\left( 4{{x}^{2}}-12x+9 \right)}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{-2{{x}^{3}}+6x-5}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)}^{2}}}=\frac{-2\left[ {{\left( x-\frac{3}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \right]}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-3x+2 \right)}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{-2\left[ {{\left( x-\frac{3}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \right]}{{{[\left( x-1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)]}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

now $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is always less than zero as numerator and denominator are both positive

i.e. ${{\left( x-\frac{3}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{1}{4}>0\And {{(x-1)}^{2}}{{(x-2)}^{2}}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is strictly decreasing

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ This also means that there will be no maxima & minima as it is strictly decreasing. $\text{y}$ is decreasing in $\left( -\infty ,1 \right)\cup \left( 1,2 \right)\cup \left( 2,\infty \right)$

Step 4: 

Asymptotes: Since denominator could be zero there will be asymptotes for those points.

And in case denominator becoming zero there are vertical asymptotes at $\text{x}=1$ $\And 2$

We can also prove it

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\underset{x\to {{1}^{+}}}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left[ \frac{2x-3}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)} \right]=+\infty \\ {} & ~\underset{x\to {{1}^{-}}}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left[ \frac{2x-3}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)} \right]=-\infty \\ \end{array}$

hence $\text{x}=1$ is an asymptote

Similarly we can prove it for $\text{x}=2$

Now we can draw the curve

Now we know that the function is a decreasing one so we will draw decreasing curve.

See all these curves are decreasing functions and have been drawn in their respective regions according to step-3.
